topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something unimportant. It was a notification from the admissions office, linking to a sample essay, supposedly from a highly selective university. I clicked it, the screen lighting up with perfectly structured prose about overcoming adversity through obscure volunteer work. I skimmed the words, my eyes drifting over the carefully crafted anecdotes. There was nothing that snagged my attention, no spark of desire to engage. I closed the tab without saving anything, the feeling remaining flat, like lukewarm water in my hand. I scrolled through my own drafts, a collection of meandering thoughts about a summer job and a poorly executed debate, and felt a familiar shrug settle over my shoulders. It was just another piece of required homework, filed away under 'things I will never bother perfecting.'

[story 2]

I found the file tucked deep within a folder labeled 'Miscellaneous.' It was an essay, polished to a mirror sheen, detailing a profound moment of self-discovery. I read it once, then twice, the words washing over me without registering any genuine emotional response. My fingers hovered over the mouse, then moved to the delete button. Why bother? The whole process felt tedious, an obligation I was currently choosing to bypass. I leaned back in my chair, the worn fabric creaking under my weight. The excitement I’d expected—that frantic urge to rewrite, to inject some unique, burning passion into my own narrative—was simply absent. It was as if the essay existed in a vacuum, perfectly formed and utterly devoid of anything that resonated with my current state of being. I clicked 'delete,' and the screen returned to its default, unremarkable state.

[story 3]

The link appeared on my desktop, an artifact from a late-night search. It was an application essay, lauded by the university as a model of introspection and drive. I opened it, the font sharp and authoritative. I read the paragraphs, noting the sophisticated vocabulary and the seamless transition between ideas. My gaze moved across the text, assessing its structure, but my mind remained stubbornly unengaged. I tapped my pen against the desk twice, a small, sharp sound in the quiet room. There was no pull toward emulation, no sudden surge of ambition to emulate the writer’s journey. It was just information—a demonstration of what some people deemed worthy. I minimized the window, the sense of obligation dissolving into a quiet resignation. It was fine. It was just an example.
